title: Anna Karenina
author: Leo Tolstoy

timeline.span: 1872–1876 (approximate; the novel is undated - only Anna's death in May and Vronsky's departure for the 1876 Serbian war are firmly fixed)

overture: Every journey in Tolstoy's novel, drawn on the map of imperial Russia - two love stories running opposite ways along one dead-straight railway: Anna's, from a snowbound platform to a train she will not survive; and Levin's, from a country estate to a hard-won peace.
sources: Constance Garnett translation (Project Gutenberg #1399); Nabokov, Lectures on Russian Literature; railway histories of the Nikolaevsky and Nizhny Novgorod lines.

regions.name: The two capitals & the line between

characters.name: Anna Karenina
characters.role: Karenin's wife; her love for Vronsky is the tragic spine of the book.

exit.note: One Sunday in May, half-mad with jealousy and morphine, Anna takes the Nizhny line out to intercept Vronsky and throws herself under a goods wagon on the platform at Obiralovka. Part 8 then runs on through a whole further summer, which is why the day has to carry this and the chapter cannot.

characters.name: Count Vronsky
characters.role: A cavalry officer; Anna's lover, and the ruin of them both.

exit.note: Koznyshev watches Vronsky board the volunteer train at Moscow, ruined by a toothache and by wanting no more of his life, and that is the last the novel sees of him. Tolstoy never brings him to Serbia and never says whether he lived, so the map draws the direction of departure and then lets him go rather than post him alive at Belgrade for the rest of the run.

characters.name: Konstantin Levin
characters.role: A landowner and Tolstoy's self-portrait; the book's moral counterweight.

characters.name: Kitty Shcherbatskaya
characters.role: Dolly's young sister; refuses Levin for Vronsky, then finds her way back.

characters.name: Stiva Oblonsky
characters.role: Anna's genial, spendthrift brother; the shuttle between the two plots.

locations.name: Moscow
locations.note: Named as itself. The Oblonsky marriage crisis, Levin's courtship, and the ball where Vronsky throws Kitty over for Anna.
locations.story: Where the book opens, in confusion: Stiva Oblonsky has been caught in an affair, and his sister Anna comes from Petersburg to make peace. Levin comes to propose to Kitty and is refused; at the ball, Vronsky abandons Kitty for Anna, and everything begins.
locations.quote: Happy families are all alike; every unhappy family is unhappy in its own way.

image.caption: The Kremlin above the Moskva river - Moscow, where Kitty refuses Levin and Anna first dances with Vronsky.
image.credit: Photochrom, c.1890s · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Petersburg
locations.note: Named as itself; the seat of the Karenin household and of the high society that slowly closes against Anna.
locations.story: The Karenins' world, and society's tribunal. Here Anna lives with her husband and her son; here the affair becomes a scandal, Karenin refuses her a divorce, and the drawing-rooms shut their doors.

image.caption: Nevsky Prospekt towards the Admiralty spire - Petersburg, the Karenins' city and society's cold court on Anna's fall.

locations.name: the station in the snowstorm
locations.note: The only substantial night-halt midway on the Nikolaevsky line; Nabokov identifies it as the snowstorm station, though Tolstoy never names it.
locations.story: The hinge of the affair. Fleeing Moscow by the night train, Anna steps onto a blizzard-swept platform for air - and Vronsky is there, having followed her, to say that wherever she is, is where he must be.

image.caption: Bologoye station on the Petersburg–Moscow line, where Anna steps out into the snowstorm and finds Vronsky has followed her.

locations.name: Krasnoe Selo
locations.note: The imperial summer military camp and racecourse south-west of Petersburg.
locations.story: The officers' steeplechase. Vronsky rides his mare Frou-Frou to the front, then breaks her back with a clumsy movement in the saddle - and Anna, watching, cries out so nakedly that her husband beside her knows the truth.
locations.imageBlank: The scene here is the officers' steeplechase where Vronsky breaks Frou-Frou's back; the racecourse went unphotographed, and the surviving period views are all of the railway station - neither what the novel means nor a third platform this book needs.

locations.name: Pokrovskoye
locations.note: Levin's estate - invented, but Yasnaya Polyana in all but name: the mowing, farming and estate scenes are drawn from Tolstoy's own Tula country. Positioned at Yasnaya Polyana, south of Tula.
locations.story: Levin's ground, and the book's answer to Anna's ruin. He mows with the peasants, wrestles with faith and farming, brings Kitty home as his bride, sees his son born, and finds, at the last, a reason to live.

image.caption: Mowing time - Levin's estate is unlocated Tula country; this is the haymaking he takes up alongside his peasants. Grigory Myasoyedov, 'Mowing Time', 1887.
image.credit: Grigory Myasoyedov, 1887 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Vozdvizhenskoye
locations.note: Vronsky's estate - invented. Tied in the text both to the Kashin election-world and to a day's drive from the Oblonsky country; placed between the Pokrovskoye cluster and Kashin as a compromise.
locations.story: Vronsky and Anna's grand modern establishment - the model hospital, the English ways, the wealth. Dolly visits and sees, under the polish, how little happiness it holds, and how the affair has begun to crack.
locations.imageBlank: The only period image to hand is Polenov's 'Grandmother's Garden' - a faded, decaying gentry house, the very opposite of Vronsky's aggressively modern new estate with its hospital and English fittings; a blank is more honest than the wrong mood.

locations.name: Kashin
locations.note: A real district town in Tver Governorate, but Tolstoy promotes it to a fictional provincial capital - no 'Kashin Governorate' existed. The elections are modelled on the noble/zemstvo elections Tolstoy knew.
locations.story: The provincial nobility elections. Levin comes hoping to believe in public duty and leaves disillusioned; Vronsky is there too, and the two men who love the same women circle each other in the crowd.

image.caption: Kashin, the provincial town where the nobility gather for the elections that draw Levin and Vronsky into the same hall - photographed by Prokudin-Gorsky, c.1910.
image.credit: Sergei Prokudin-Gorsky, c.1910 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: the little German watering-place
locations.note: Garnett calls it only 'the little German watering-place'; the Russian 'Soden' is universally identified as Bad Soden am Taunus, near Frankfurt.
locations.story: Kitty's convalescence after Vronsky's humiliation. Among the invalids and the pious she meets Varenka and tries, and fails, at saintly self-denial - and slowly gets well enough to go home and, at last, to Levin.

image.caption: The drinking-hall in the spa park at Bad Soden am Taunus, where Kitty takes the waters to mend - this view a little later, c.1920.
image.credit: Postcard, c.1920 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: a small Italian town
locations.note: Tolstoy names no city - the text sets it apart from 'Venice, Rome, and Naples', yet Vronsky rents a palazzo hung with a Tintoretto. Placed on Venice as a defensible Veneto anchor; the town itself is unnamed.
locations.story: The brief idyll abroad. Free of Russia, Anna and Vronsky are happy for a while; he plays at painting until the real painter Mihailov shames the dabbling - and paints a portrait of Anna truer than Vronsky's own. Then the boredom starts.

image.caption: Tolstoy leaves the town unnamed; Anna and Vronsky's Italian months are placed at Venice - the Grand Canal, photochrom, c.1890s.
image.credit: Photochrom, c.1890s · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Obiralovka
locations.note: A real station on the Nizhny Novgorod line east of Moscow (renamed Zheleznodorozhny in 1939) - a different line from the Petersburg railway.
locations.story: The end. Half-mad with jealousy, Anna takes a train out to intercept Vronsky and, on the platform, throws herself beneath a goods wagon - the crushed watchman of the first chapter come round at last for her.
locations.quote: “And the light by which she had read the book filled with troubles, falsehoods, sorrow, and evil, flared up more brightly than ever before, lighted up for her all that had been in darkness, flickered, began to grow dim, and was quenched forever.”

image.caption: A station on a Russian line - Obiralovka itself went unphotographed; this platform (Borodino, c.1910) stands for the suburban stop where Anna steps under the train.
image.credit: Sergei Prokudin-Gorsky, c.1910 · Wikimedia Commons · public domain

locations.name: Serbia
locations.note: Named. Vronsky leaves as a volunteer for the Serbo-Turkish war of 1876 - Tolstoy's contemporary, and pointedly sceptical, coda. Belgrade is only the anchor coordinate for a direction of departure.
locations.story: Vronsky's exit. Broken by Anna's death and courting his own, he boards a volunteer train for the Serbian front, seeing his squadron off toward a war Tolstoy plainly doubts. Koznyshev watches him go - the last we see of him.

image.caption: Serbian troops with a field gun, 1876 - the war Vronsky rides south to join once Anna is dead.

movements.note: Anna travels from Petersburg to reconcile her brother and his wife; on the platform she meets Vronsky, and a watchman is crushed under the train.
movements.routeNote: The Nikolaevsky (Petersburg–Moscow) Railway, opened 1851 - the ruler-straight line the novel runs on.
movements.routeSource: Saint Petersburg–Moscow railway history; Nabokov's commentary.

movements.note: Anna flees home after the ball; Vronsky boards the same train and, at the blizzard-swept midnight halt, declares himself.
movements.routeNote: The Nikolaevsky line again; Bologoye is the midway night-stop of the snowstorm scene.
movements.routeSource: Nabokov identifies Bologoye as the halt.

movements.note: Levin comes up from the country to propose to Kitty - and is refused, for she is waiting on Vronsky.
movements.routeNote: Rail up from the Tula country to Moscow.
movements.routeSource: The imperial Russian railway network, 1870s (Nikolaevsky and Moscow–Kursk–Kiev lines).

movements.note: Rebuffed, he goes home and throws himself into the land.
movements.routeSource: The imperial Russian railway network, 1870s (Nikolaevsky and Moscow–Kursk–Kiev lines).

movements.note: Vronsky rides out to the officers' steeplechase at Krasnoe Selo.
movements.routeNote: A short line south-west from Petersburg to the military camp.
movements.routeSource: The imperial Russian railway network, 1870s (Nikolaevsky and Moscow–Kursk–Kiev lines).

movements.note: He returns to the city after the fall; Anna's betrayal of herself hangs between them.
Anna drives out from Petersburg to the races, taking her seat in the stand beside Karenin.
The race over and her face already read, she drives back to the city beside her husband.
Ill and humiliated after Vronsky, Kitty is taken to the German waters to recover.
movements.routeNote: A reconstructed 1870s route through Warsaw and Berlin to the Frankfurt spa belt.
movements.routeSource: Bradshaw's Continental Guide (1870s); Soden = Bad Soden am Taunus.

movements.note: Levin returns to Moscow, and this time, in a game of chalk-letters, Kitty accepts him.
movements.routeSource: The imperial Russian railway network, 1870s (Nikolaevsky and Moscow–Kursk–Kiev lines).

movements.note: Well again, Kitty comes home to Moscow - and to the second chance she will give Levin.
movements.routeSource: Reconstructed from period railway guides (Bradshaw, Baedeker); estate positions conjectured from the text.

movements.note: Free at last of Karenin, Anna leaves Russia with Vronsky for the happiness of abroad.
movements.routeNote: A reconstructed 1870s rail route - Petersburg–Warsaw, the Warsaw–Vienna line, then the Alpine Südbahn to the Adriatic; Tolstoy names only 'a small Italian town'.

movements.note: They come back to Russia; Anna makes a secret, wrenching visit to her son on his birthday, and society snubs her at the opera.
movements.routeNote: The return over the same reconstructed period rail route.

movements.note: Married in Moscow, Levin brings Kitty home to Pokrovskoye to begin their life.
movements.routeSource: The imperial Russian railway network, 1870s (Nikolaevsky and Moscow–Kursk–Kiev lines).

movements.note: The lovers withdraw to Vronsky's country estate, where Dolly visits and sees the cracks already spreading.
movements.routeNote: Rail south from Petersburg then carriage to the estate; the estate's position is itself a best guess.
movements.routeSource: Estate location conjectured from the text.

movements.note: Levin goes to the provincial elections at Kashin, hoping to believe in public duty.
movements.routeNote: Rail and road north to the fictionalised provincial capital.
movements.routeSource: Reconstructed from period railway guides (Bradshaw, Baedeker); estate positions conjectured from the text.

movements.note: Disillusioned by the elections, he hurries back to Kitty.
movements.routeSource: Reconstructed from period railway guides (Bradshaw, Baedeker); estate positions conjectured from the text.

movements.note: Anna and Vronsky move to Moscow, where jealousy, boredom and morphine corrode what is left.
movements.routeNote: Carriage to the railway, then the line to Moscow.
movements.routeSource: Reconstructed from period railway guides (Bradshaw, Baedeker); estate positions conjectured from the text.

movements.note: One Sunday in May, half-mad, Anna takes a train out toward Obiralovka to intercept Vronsky - and throws herself beneath a goods wagon.
movements.routeNote: The Nizhny Novgorod line east out of Moscow - a different railway from the Petersburg trunk.
movements.routeSource: Obiralovka (now Zheleznodorozhny) on the Nizhny Novgorod line.

movements.note: Stiva shuttles to Petersburg to hunt a well-paid post and to press Karenin, in vain, for Anna's divorce.
movements.routeNote: The Nikolaevsky line - Stiva is forever crossing it between the two plots.
movements.routeSource: The imperial Russian railway network, 1870s (Nikolaevsky and Moscow–Kursk–Kiev lines).

movements.note: After Anna's death, broken and seeking his own, Vronsky leaves with a volunteer squadron for the Serbian war.
movements.routeNote: A reconstructed overland route toward the Balkans - there was no through railway to Belgrade in 1876; the real volunteers went by rail to the southern railheads and on by Danube steamer.
movements.routeSource: 1876 Russian-volunteer travel accounts; Serbian railway history (Belgrade station opened 1884).

story.title: An unhappy house
story.narration: The novel opens in an unhappy house. Stepan Oblonsky - 'Stiva', a genial, spendthrift Moscow official - has been caught out in an affair, and his home is in uproar. He wires his married sister in Petersburg to come and make peace.
That sister is Anna Karenina: wife of a cold and eminent Petersburg statesman, mother of a small boy, and the most captivating woman in Russian society. She sets out at once.

On the night train she falls in with an old countess - and meets the countess's son, the cavalry officer Count Vronsky. As they pull in, a watchman is crushed under the wheels. 'An evil omen,' Anna says.
Away in the country, another man is screwing up his courage. Konstantin Levin - shy, earnest, the book's moral heart - loves young Kitty Shcherbatskaya, and travels up to Moscow to ask for her hand.
He makes his offer, and Kitty refuses him. She is waiting, she believes, for Vronsky - and cannot see that Vronsky is already lost.

story.title: The ball
story.narration: At the great ball, Vronsky has eyes for no one but Anna. He throws Kitty over without a word; Kitty watches her hopes go out like a lamp; and Anna, frightened by what she has stirred in herself, flees for home.
Rebuffed, Levin goes back to his estate and throws himself into the land - the mowing, the farming, the plain hard work of the fields - to forget her.

Anna takes the night train home; Vronsky, helpless, boards the same train. At a blizzard-swept midnight halt she steps out for air, and there he is - to tell her that wherever she is, is where he must be.
We leave the lovers a while for Kitty. Heartsick and humiliated after Vronsky, she falls ill; her family carries her off to the German waters to recover.
At the little spa she meets the saintly Varenka, tries and fails at pious self-denial, and slowly, quietly, begins to get well.
Anna drives out from Petersburg to the racecourse at Krasnoe Selo, taking her place in the stand with her husband Karenin beside her to watch the officers' steeplechase.
Back in Russia the affair deepens. Vronsky, garrisoned near Petersburg, rides in the summer officers' steeplechase at Krasnoe Selo, with Anna watching from the stand.

He drives his beloved mare Frou-Frou to the front of the field - then, with one clumsy movement in the saddle, breaks her back. And Anna cries out so nakedly that her husband, beside her, knows everything.
He rides back to the city a marked man. The secret is a secret no longer, and Karenin begins to think of what a husband must do.
The race over and her cry already heard, Anna drives back to Petersburg beside Karenin, the silence between them heavier now than the crowd they have left behind.
Kitty, well again, comes home to Moscow - older, and wiser about her own heart.
The long cure over, she returns to the city where, without knowing it, she is about to be given a second chance.
And Levin, who has never once stopped loving her, comes up to Moscow again.
This time, in a game of chalk-letters at the card table - each writing only the first letters of the words - they understand each other completely, and are engaged.
Married in Moscow, Levin brings Kitty home to Pokrovskoye to begin their life on the land.

story.title: Levin's country
story.narration: There Levin farms and argues and doubts. His brother Nikolai dies in a shabby hotel - Kitty nursing him with a courage that astonishes her husband - and Levin, shaken by death, gropes after a faith he cannot yet name.

Back to Anna. After a childbirth that nearly kills her, and a strange deathbed forgiveness between the three of them, she finds she can bear the lie no longer - and leaves husband and son to go abroad with Vronsky.
They flee Russia for Italy, and for a little while, free of it all, they are happy; Vronsky even plays at painting, until a real painter shames the dabbling.
But the happiness sours into boredom, and they come back. Anna makes a secret, wrenching visit to her son on his birthday; that same week, society shuts its doors in her face at the opera.

The lovers withdraw to Vronsky's grand country estate. Dolly - Anna's sister-in-law, the careworn wife Stiva betrays - comes to stay, and sees, beneath the wealth and the model hospital, how little is left between them.
Levin, meanwhile, goes to the provincial nobility elections at Kashin, hoping to find some public duty he can believe in.
The elections turn out to be all vanity and manoeuvre, and he comes away more disillusioned than before.
He hurries home to Kitty, and to the child that is coming.
Anna and Vronsky move to Moscow, but jealousy, idleness and morphine are eating the love alive - every day a quarrel and a reconciliation, each one thinner than the last.
Her brother Stiva shuttles up to Petersburg - angling for a soft, well-paid post, and pressing Karenin, for Anna's sake, to grant the divorce.
But Karenin, wrapped now in mysticism and a scheming countess, refuses. There will be no divorce, and no way out.
And so, back in Moscow, Anna's whole world narrows to a single unbearable point: the certainty, true or not, that Vronsky has stopped loving her.

One Sunday in May, half-mad, she drives to the station and takes a train out toward Obiralovka to intercept him. On the platform, as a goods wagon passes, she throws herself beneath it - the omen of the first page come round at last.
Two months pass. Vronsky, broken and courting his own death, leaves with a volunteer squadron for the war in Serbia.
Levin's half-brother Koznyshev, the cool intellectual, sees him off from the Moscow platform, grey and finished - the last we ever see of him, riding toward a war the book plainly doubts.
And the book gives its last word not to Anna's ruin, but to Levin - out in the country, and the peace he has been groping toward all along.

story.title: The quiet answer
story.narration: At Pokrovskoye, holding his newborn son under a vast night sky, Levin comes at last to his quiet answer: that to live for goodness, without needing to explain why, is enough. There Tolstoy leaves us.
